sentences.text: Yeah.
We had it like an amphitheater hall where we used to have our meetings.
And there was always a guard standing, and if somebody suspicious was passing through the street, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he gave us a special whistle, and we all toned down and kept quiet.
And if something was real kind of questionable, there was a shed right close by the-- that hall, where we all went into that shed.
And from this shed, there was an open board going into another section that was like another block facing another street, and the people just dispersed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Oh, quite a few occasions.
Because sometimes the German people were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coming in and walking through the ghetto.

And we didn't even want the Jewish policemen should know too much about our activities, because sometimes their allegiance was also not too much to us, and we were afraid that some information will leak out.
Regretfully and sadly, I hate to talk about it, but there were-- in every community, there are good people and bad people, and Jews are no exception.
There were some that thought that this is their glory and power, when they had that uniform, and they had a rubber stick.
The Germans didn't trust them with a gun, so they used it sometimes when they shouldn't have.
But people are shortsighted.
They gave all kind of orders.
They wanted to be listened to and so on.
And they could have been more gentle.
But these were exceptions.
Other people knew to be in line.
But of course you have to be careful for such exceptions.
We were also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trying to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not to cause too much commotion in town.
Because this was the auspices of a formal education, but actually we had underground spiritual activities there that we didn't want the other people to know about.
I was in a very kind of fortunate situation.
That little apartment that we moved into in that little house was bordering.
The house was in the corner, and then the fence from that house we lived in was bordering with the garden.
So even though the curfew, I could go over that in the evening, and we could work at the-- the vegetables and so on.
And in the same house downstairs lived another of our members, [NON-ENGLISH], and he used to come and help, too.
So this was very good.
Because even if you lived across the street, you couldn't come and cross the street after curfew.
But he and I could sometimes, when necessary, go over.
And we had to take the water from the pump and pump it in the buckets and-- how's it called that you--
Pardon?
Sprinkling cans.
And we were sprinkling the-- the garden, taking the water from the pump.
The majority of our members in my group-- there were three age groups.
There were-- was one that were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 18 to 20-some.

There was-- we were a group that was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] during the war, we were already 14, 15, and 16, and we organized-- in fact, our group organized a group of 12-year-olds during the war, a younger group that we were leading.
In fact, I had-- and a friend of mine had two [NON-ENGLISH].
We called them [NON-ENGLISH], those things.
And-- and they were called 'dror' and 'la'or,' which means-- dror is freedom, and la'or to light.
And you know, just by the names was a signal of hope.
And since those young people didn't have any school, not only did we provide them with education in groups-- because our group of 50 people was teaching [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 500 children.
Each of us was teaching 10 or 12, or depending [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- in the age group or something.
But not only did we teach those 12-year-olds, but we were-- we were supplying them with a spiritual outlet and so forth.
And they also met in our garden.
The oldest group didn't meet in our garden, just our group and those younger people that we organized.
We had a self help if one of our members got sick or something.
The produce from the garden were used mainly to support our sick members.
A lot of them had TB.
And the situation was very, very rough in the ghetto, due to the fact that they weren't giving us any rations.
People were starving.
You could find swollen people from hunger on the street, and it was just starting to get hopeless, the situation with the great hunger.
In our ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to begin with there were 15,000, 12,000.
Later on, there were 20,000 because from surrounding towns.
And 25.
And we were all very much bunched up and gathered together.
And the hygienic circumstances weren't so great.
And as a result, a lot of sickness and typhus was in the ghetto.
Pardon?
If you have 10, 15 people living in an apartment that doesn't have toilet facilities and doesn't have a wash room, at best, as you know it here in America, you bring up the water to wash yourself.
And where you are not-- before the war, people that did not have the facilities at home could wash themselves in-- in sinks and with water or go to special bath houses.
Like a lot of people, at least once a week, they went to the hot baths and the steam baths or something like that.
But these were not the circumstances in-- in the ghetto.
And besides--
The best they could under the circumstances, you know?
But if you were-- were 10 people to a small apartment, and some of them weren't even related, you don't free-- feel free to undress yourself and-- and wash yourself.
You don't get the privacy or something.
So they just-- it was a lot of superficial, the best under the circumstances.
The-- the washing facility of clothes weren't sufficient.
And the worst part was there wasn't any fresh fruit and vegetable sufficient.
There was nothing sufficient.
And we were, in our ghetto, a lot of civilian Germans lived around my home.
Now, civilian Germans are people with a German background that lived in Poland and still kept their culture and their-- they were not Catholics.
They were [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] evangelicals and so on or Protestants.
And they kept their culture, and they were like the Jews in Poland.
They were Germans in Poland.

But when the Germans occupied my hometown, they all became big shots. 12-Year-old [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] children of the volksdeutchen-- they were called volksdeutchen-- were belonging to Hitler jungen, and they were given guns, and they used to target shoot into the ghetto just to practice their shooting.
And people got really killed.
They were falling.
In fact, one of my close girlfriends was tied to a pole, and-- and they were target shooting to her.
And actually, she got-- and such incidents were not rare.
Then another thing, the hunger was so great, the majority of the food came in smuggled in.
The people dug a hole under the fence.
They went out-- mostly children smuggled out-- out of the ghetto.
And they bought or-- or they went to some houses to get some food if they knew somebody or so on.
And on the way back, many of them got caught.
And every day, there were dead children.
Children, I mean 10, 12-year-old, even 8-year-olds that went out.
And they [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] brought in whatever food they could to share with their family.
And many of them got killed in such a way.
But you die from starvation, so you die from a bullet.
And there wasn't much choice.
The people that died, who got-- they collected them on-- on a wagon, and they took them to the Jewish cemetery and buried them in mass graves or something.
And if there just was a few that particular day-- which was a rarity-- and they just allowed the very immediate family to follow, and then under the police supervision, bring them back.
This was in the beginning.
Later on, [SCOFFS] they didn't even give that courtesy, they just took away the bodies, and who knows where.
Both life and death was very, very difficult in the ghetto.
In fact, this was so ugly that people were holding on to life not to die in such an impersonal way.
Because you know, you get shot, you get pulled by your hair and by your hands.
There was no respect for the living and no respect for the death.
It was just--
It was very difficult.
I remember, one time I saw a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a father and a child lying in the street.
And the child was swollen, and the father was asking for a piece of bread or something to eat.
There-- there wasn't much to share.
But anyhow, I gave the father a little bread for the child.
And the child did not let me give that back to the father.
He wanted it alone, because he was afraid that maybe the father will eat it up instead of he eat it up.
The hunger was so great that some very unusual things happened that you cannot imagine.
Pardon?
About what?
About my own hunger.
As I said, as long as I was around my house in my hometown, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had some belongings that we kept selling and for the money buy some-- some food.
Nobody really ate to be full, but just to sustain themselves.
And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as I mentioned, our business was taken over by a troy handler, which means a trustee from the Germans.
He had no idea about how to run a business like that, what to do with it.
So as a result, he took my father and my brother to work for him.
He was a volksdeutscher who he was from a different town, from Lietsmannstadt, from Lodz, which was in the-- in the Reich.
And we were in the gubernatorial part of occupation.
And every Sunday-- for Sunday, he went to his family, and he sometimes took-- he was a man that besides taking care of his own business, he liked to make some extra money.
So when he went to Lietsmannstadt, we used to give him four coats.
They were not allowed for the Jews to wear.
We needed to turn them in to the Germans.
But we didn't turn them all in.
So sometimes we used to even buy them off from other people and give it to him, and he took it to Lietsmannstadt.
And there he exchanged it for some money, and that money enabled some of the other people to buy some food on the black market.
Because those children or the people that smuggled it in, they not only smuggled it in for themselves, but they smuggled it in to-- to sell.
As I say, thousands die, a few survived.
And it's hard to say why the few survived, but it was just luck and maybe resourcefulness or something.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Circumstances or something that guide.
Even though they took our business away and everything without any compensation or something, but somehow he was guided we could exchange some goods with him, and he helped us.
In turn, we could help somebody else too.
So this is a-- And I was usually a very thin child.
When I was a little, I-- I was a very small eater.
In fact, my mother was always afraid that I'm going to get sick, so they used to carry the food around me and-- and stuff me with it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It came to a point that let's see chocolate milk.
They forced me to drink it.
Until after the war, I did not like.
During-- during the war, if I would've gotten it, I would've loved it.
But till the war broke out, I did not like any chocolate milk or something like that.
I remember when I was already 13 years old, I used to go to school, and there was a poor woman sitting there in front of the school.
And I gave her my lunch before I even walked in to the school.
And somebody told it to my mother, so later on, they used to send the maid to school, and I had to eat in the teachers' room with my--
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow, even during the war, I need to bring out, my mind wasn't so around food.
And my body maybe didn't need so much food as other people needed.
And when I-- the war kept going on and during the camps that I lived in, I never at up my piece of bread till I did not get another piece of bread.
And I said, 'I have bread.
I just don't want to eat it.' And that didn't made me so wild hungry, because people were so hungry, like worse than any animal you can-- hungry animal you can imagine.
How did you take care of your garden of vegetables?
As I say, the garden of vegetables just wasn't my garden.
This was the group's.
That was distributed strictly between our sick members.
In the night was a curfew, and nobody could go.
And during the day, we were around.
And besides, the group of the people that were there were, I have to say, honorable young people with ideals.
And with-- and it was.
There wasn't so much for everybody to go around.
This was mainly to prolong the life of those that were weak and those that were sick.
There was mainly used for that purpose.
There wasn't so much of it either.
There were tomatoes and carrots and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] other vegetables [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that-- that you could make a-- a vegetable soup.
We had also a self-help kitchen that we used to take over some food to-- to our friends and that are specifically in dire need.
And some of the people, they, due to sickness, their parents passed away, and they were sick themselves.
And it was a very rough situation in the ghetto.
It is nothing that people can here compare it with.
It was worse than any jail you can imagine because you was just locked in there.
You couldn't go, and you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] couldn't be your own master.
And you did not have any source of food.
Here, in a jail, you get three meals a day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But this was not the situation over there.

We, our group, got the news, because now and then, we got a contact person that smuggled into the ghetto-- in fact, her name was Minka Liebeskind, and she was the sister of our leader in Krakow, and his name was Dolek Liebeskind.
Libeskind.
L-I-B-E-S-K-I-N-D.
Liebeskind.
Yeah.
And he was the leader of our organization.
And-- and we also had a-- a newsletter that they were making there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Gusta Davidson Draenger and Shimshon Shoemaker Draenger were writing the paper.
And this was called This was 'The Fighting Chalutz.' And they were also-- The Fighting Chalutz is the fighting pioneer.
And they were also-- let's see, in 1941.
And so they were reaching out to other Zionist movements, and they joined them together to have both a spiritual and an armed resistance.
We had in our-- in our movement in Krakow, they were soliciting people, and they were from the movement to come over to Krakow to the headquarters, and they supplied them with Aryan papers.
In fact, Gusta Davidson and Shimich Draenger were specialists in making those counterfeit-- the Aryan papers.
They were so good that sometimes the people did not recognize that it wasn't their [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] signature.

The forms were stolen from the German office by people that were working there, Jewish people that were working there as cleaning crews, and they stole-- from our group-- and they stole those papers and supplied them.
And they made the Aryan papers.
And Minka Liebeskind, when she came, she brought those papers to certain people.
You had to have good looks and a good Polish [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] pronunciation in order to make use of those papers.

So you could have all the courage, but if you had black hair and black eyes and-- and features that were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here they would look like Italians and like Spanish, like French people.
But if you had those looks in Poland, since those other nationalities didn't live there, you were unmistakably a Jew, and you took your life in your hand whether you had Aryan papers or not.
And specifically, if you were a young man, besides the bad looks, if they could disrobe you and see that you were circumcised, you were in real trouble.
And-- and there were a lot of people, I'm sorry to say, Polish people, instead of realizing that the Germans were our common enemy, they still denounced the Jews.
And you had to be more careful sometimes for the Pole than you were for the German, because they could kind of smell you out more.
